summary: The main claim is: The problem is that the agent will probably predict that this text is not caused by aliens, but by the program it is running on.
We will construct an agent that communicates with a human over text.

top_comment.author: Joseph Bloom
top_comment.text:
"In this post, I'll present a way to turn LLMs into agents such that we can approximately model them as a utility maximizer."

If this works it would be very dangerous and kind of thing we would want to avoid. We're very lucky current systems are as poorly agentic as they are.
top_comment.claim: The comment's point: "In this post, I'll present a way to turn LLMs into agents such that we can approximately model them as a utility maximizer." If this works it would be very dangerous and kind of thing we would want to avoid.

summary: The main claim is: That human level AIs are arriving seems much less controversial and they are themselves dangerous by virtue of the reasoning/scenario laid out above.
The main claim is: And thus it may be the case that for further improvements OOM more new data will need to be generated.
Not only this, but so far newer generations of AI have required more data than previous generations.

The main claim is: I think the author of the Simulators post understands the point I make in this section – for instance, they state: This is true for GPT, where the text “initial condition” of most training samples severely underdetermines the real-world pr…

Instead, I think we should just directly reason about predictors and think in terms of questions such as ‘what would the model predict for the next token?’ In this post, I won’t make arguments that I think are strong enough to decisively j…
The main claim is: In each of these areas, there are research questions relevant to RaD-AI.
I'm co-organizing a workshop on a really interesting topic that's very relevant for AI safety.
The main claim is: -- So even an AI with "human-level" intelligence will want to get more computing power, have safeguards against being shut down, and obtain resources like money and contacts.
A normal human is more concerned with comfort, more afraid of hard work, and more complacent to do what everyone else is doing: not much.
The main claim is: Ask a language model to write prompts for short stories 2.
Repeat until the model is really good at writing short stories.

The main claim is: However, convincing the top researchers at the most advanced groups that AGI is incredibly dangerous and that we should try to keep AIs as narrow as possible until we're really confident that we know what they will do seems more plausible.
Realistically, I think suggestions to stop AI development completely at these places or to get world governments to enact effective regulations are doomed to fail.

The main claim is: So we need to both solve the outer alignment problem , ensuring the base objective is aligned with human values, and the inner alignment problem , ensuring that the mesa-objective is the same as the base objective.
And that this will likely give us good feedback on which alignment techniques actually work, make current systems safer, and make discontinuities in capabilities less likely.
The main claim is: Besides the societal benefits from truthful AI, building truthful AI systems can also help with AI alignment: 1.
This seems distinctly good and I think would in fact reduce x-risk!

The main claim is: In some sense this model “knows” quite a lot about both Klingon and English, and so it should be able to read a sentence in one language, understand it, and then express the same idea in the other language.
Right now I don’t think we have any scalable strategies to this kind of problem; I think it’s a core open question for alignment.
The main claim is: The single possibility state must be isolated, so we can mark that down in our graph.
Sometimes you advantage one AU at the cost of another, moving through the state space towards the best possibilities for one goal and away from the best possibilities for another goal.
